reasoning: The Lean statement correctly formalizes the claim. `hz : ‖z‖ = 1` places z on the unit circle (in ℂ, the norm equals the complex modulus). The partial sum `s n = ∑ i ∈ range n, z^(i+1)/(i+1)` ranges i from 0 to n-1, so k = i+1 goes from 1 to n, correctly giving partial sums of ∑_{k=1}^∞ z^k/k (starting from k=1 is the natural interpretation to avoid division by zero). The existence of z₀ with `Tendsto s atTop (𝓝 z₀)` is precisely the definition of series convergence. The iff `↔ z ≠ 1` captures both directions of "converges except at z = 1": convergence at every z ≠ 1 on the unit circle (Dirichlet's test) AND divergence at z = 1 (harmonic series) — the "except z = 1" phrasing in the informal statement naturally implies both directions.
